evals.prompt: Our SaaS is genuinely good but conversions are flat. Everyone says we should cut the price or add more features. What should we do?
evals.expected_output: Should restate the problem in human terms rather than as a price/feature metric. Should name the 'logical' fix (cut price / add features) and flag it as the expensive, saturated, competitor-default path. Should stay in the psychology lever and route to a specific sub-skill (reframing if the value isn't understood, costly-signaling if trust is the issue, counterintuitive-tests to validate). Should resist immediately recommending a discount. Should reference the idea that the perception is cheaper to change than the substance.
assertions: Reframes the problem in human/perceptual terms
Identifies the logical fix and treats it as a trap, not the answer
Routes to a specific sub-skill (reframing / costly-signaling / counterintuitive-tests / experience-design)
Does not default to cutting price or adding features

evals.prompt: how do we make people actually want this? it doesn't make sense but our weird competitor is winning
evals.expected_output: Should trigger on casual phrasing. Should invoke 'the opposite of a good idea can be a good idea' and 'doesn't have to make sense to work.' Should diagnose in human terms and route — likely to counterintuitive-tests and/or costly-signaling. Should encourage a cheap behavioural test over reasoning about it.
assertions: Invokes the core thesis (opposite of a good idea / doesn't have to make sense)
Favors a cheap behavioural test over pure reasoning
